prompt: In the Duncan model education -> income -> prestige, with a direct arrow education -> prestige, which variable is exogenous?
why: A variable is exogenous when no arrow points into it, so the model takes it as given and never tries to explain it. Education has no incoming arrow; income and prestige each have at least one, so both are endogenous. See Section 16.1, Drawing a causal story: path diagrams (Definition 16.4).

prompt: Match each path-diagram term to its meaning.

pairs.meaning: at least one arrow points into it
stub arrow for every unmeasured influence

why: These five terms carry the whole grammar of a path diagram: exogenous and endogenous split variables by whether an arrow points in, the disturbance stands for everything unmeasured, recursive means no feedback loop, and a path coefficient is a standardized regression slope. See Section 16.1 (Definitions 16.2 through 16.6).

prompt: Order the steps that estimate the paths and decompose education's total effect.
steps: Standardize education, income, and prestige to mean 0 and sd 1
Regress income on education: the slope is the arrow into income
Regress prestige on education and income: the two slopes are the arrows into prestige
Multiply the coefficients along the education-income-prestige route: the indirect effect
Add the direct path and the indirect effect: education's total effect
why: Because the model is recursive, each endogenous variable's equation is fit on its own by least squares, and the standardized slopes are the path coefficients. Effects then multiply along a route and add across routes, so the indirect piece is p_IE times p_PI and the total is direct plus indirect. See Section 16.2 and Section 16.3.

prompt: Six of Duncan's occupations. Drag the line to predict prestige from education (percent finishing high school). Get the SSE meter into the green.
why: The education-to-prestige arrow is just the slope of this regression, read in standardized units as a path coefficient. Least squares picks the slope and intercept that make the sum of squared vertical gaps smallest; across these occupations prestige rises close to one point per education point. See Section 16.2 and Theorem 16.7 (standardized coefficient).

prompt: Income against prestige for six Duncan occupations. Tap the high-influence case whose prestige sits far above what its income would predict.
why: That point is the minister: only 21 percent cleared the income line, yet the job is rated highly, because a well-schooled, modestly paid calling still earns respect. Its unusual predictor value and contrary residual give it large leverage and a big Cook's distance, enough to shift the path coefficients. See Section 16.5 and @ch09-cooks-distance.

prompt: One sentence about what path analysis can claim is wrong. Tap it.

sentences.text: The direction of each arrow is an assumption justified from outside the data, not something the correlations reveal.
An unmeasured common cause of two modeled variables can bias every path coefficient.
Because the model reproduces all three correlations almost exactly, it proves education causes prestige.
Two models that differ only by a reversed arrow can fit the same correlations equally well.

why: Good fit means the data do not contradict the story, never that the story is right. An equivalent model with a reversed arrow fits the same correlations just as well, and an omitted common cause could bias every number. See Section 16.4, What path analysis cannot claim about causation.

prompt: Income correlates with prestige at 0.84, but income's direct path to prestige is only 0.46. The remaining 0.37, which equals 0.72 times 0.52, is best called the...
options: indirect effect of income on prestige
spurious part, manufactured by education as a common cause
direct effect of income on prestige
measurement error in the prestige survey
why: Income and prestige share a common cause, education, which lifts both. The product of the two paths through education (0.72 times 0.52 = 0.37) is spurious: it reflects education, not income acting on prestige. An indirect effect, by contrast, runs forward through a mediator. See Section 16.3 (Definition 16.10, spurious correlation).
